file_name: Wan et al. - 2022 - MSLANet multi-scale long attention network for skin lesion classification.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes MSLANet, a multi-scale long attention network (a deep learning model based on Convolutional Neural Networks) for skin lesion classification in dermoscopy images. It utilizes long attention mechanisms, self-supervised learning, and deep data augmentation (DDA) to classify skin lesions (specifically distinguishing melanoma from nevus) on the ISIC 2017 and SIIM-ISIC 2020 datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper proposes a novel deep learning model, MSLANet, for skin lesion classification. It details the architecture (multi-scale long attention network), methods (self-supervised learning, deep data augmentation), and presents original experimental results on the ISIC 2017 and SIIM-ISIC 2020 datasets, achieving specific performance metrics (AUC of 93.7% and 92.4% respectively). The structure includes Introduction, Methods, Experiments, and Results, which is characteristic of an original research study rather than a review.

title: MSLANet: multi-scale long attention network for skin lesion classification
brief_summary: This study proposes MSLANet, a multi-scale long attention network for classifying skin lesions in dermoscopy images to address challenges like data insufficiency and inter-class similarity. The model integrates three long attention networks to fuse multi-scale features and employs self-supervised learning with classification activation maps to locate lesions without additional annotations. To enhance generalization, a deep data augmentation strategy combining traditional methods and StyleGAN is utilized. The method was evaluated on the ISIC 2017 and SIIM-ISIC 2020 datasets using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and area under the ROC curve (AUC). MSLANet achieved a rank-1 average AUC of 93.7% on the ISIC 2017 dataset and 92.4% on the SIIM-ISIC 2020 dataset. These results demonstrate that the proposed approach outperforms state-of-the-art methods in skin lesion classification.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two primary classes in the binary classification task on ISIC 2017 and SIIM-ISIC 2020 datasets. The paper notes the difficulty in distinguishing melanoma from nevus due to inter-class similarity.

One of the two primary classes in the binary classification task on ISIC 2017 and SIIM-ISIC 2020 datasets. The paper highlights intra-class variation and inter-class similarity with melanoma as key challenges.
Evaluated in the ISIC 2017 sub-task for seborrheic keratosis classification. MSLANet achieved an AUC of 0.971 and ACC of 0.935 for this class.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion classes (Melanoma, Nevus, Seborrheic Keratosis)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification (melanoma vs. nevus) and multi-class classification (including seborrheic keratosis) on dermoscopy images. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories using metrics such as Accuracy (ACC), Sensitivity (SE), Specificity (SP), and Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC). Specifically, it addresses the ISIC 2017 skin lesion classification challenge tasks for melanoma and seborrheic keratosis, as well as the SIIM-ISIC 2020 melanoma classification challenge.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017
datasets_used.description: The International Skin Imaging Collaboration 2017 skin lesion classification dataset. It consists of dermoscopy images with gold standard diagnoses. The study also utilized an additional collection of 1800 images from the ISIC Archive for generalization purposes.

use.training: Used for training the MSLANet model (2000 images). Additional 1800 images from ISIC Archive were collected to improve generalization.
use.other: Used for self-supervised learning to obtain Classification Activation Maps (CAM) via a big dermoscopy image dataset collected from ISIC Archive.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2020
datasets_used.description: The 2020 SIIM Melanoma Classification challenge dataset. It consists of dermoscopy images for identifying melanomas using clinical context.

use.training: Used for training the MSLANet model.
use.testing: Used for testing (performance reported as AUC of 92.4%).
use.other: Used to prove the effectiveness of the proposed method on a different dataset.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using point estimates of metrics (Accuracy, AUC, Sensitivity, Specificity) without statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2017.description: Skin lesion classification dataset. Melanoma and Seborrheic Keratosis classification tasks were evaluated. The test set was not publicly available; the official training set was split into training, validation, and test sets in a 6:1:3 ratio.
ISIC 2017.notes: MSLANet achieved the highest AUC for melanoma (0.903) and seborrheic keratosis (0.971) classification, as well as the highest average AUC (0.937). It outperformed state-of-the-art methods including GP-CNN-DTEL, ARL-CNN, SSAC, SDL, and top-ranking challenge methods (#1-#5). The paper notes that MSLANet achieved an AUC > 0.9 for melanoma for the first time in this task.

SIIM-ISIC 2020.description: Melanoma classification challenge dataset. The official training set was divided into training, validation, and test sets in a 6:1:3 ratio because test labels were not publicly available.
SIIM-ISIC 2020.notes: MSLANet achieved the highest ACC (0.956) and AUC (0.924) among the compared state-of-the-art image classification methods. Its AUC was 0.029 higher than ResNet-50. The paper highlights that MSLANet achieved state-of-the-art performance without using an ensemble strategy.

architecture_used.pretraining: ImageNet pre-training was used for the backbone (ResNet50). Self-supervised learning (MoCov2) was used on a large dermoscopy dataset from the ISIC Archive to obtain Classification Activation Maps (CAM) for cropping, rather than for direct feature pretraining of the classifier.
architecture_used.input_size: 224x224 (images were resized to this standard size after augmentation and cropping)

architecture_details.structure: Composed of three Long Attention Networks (LANets) operating at different scales. Each LANet fuses multi-scale information in the downsampling process and modifies low-level features using high-level semantic information via a long attention mechanism.
architecture_details.attention_mechanism: Long attention mechanism using a mixed sigmoid function to normalize feature maps into an attention soft mask containing mixed channel and spatial attention information. 1x1 convolution is used for dimension adjustment.
architecture_details.fusion_strategy: Outputs of the three LANets are averaged (decision fusion). Five dropouts are added at the end of each LANet, and their average is taken as the final output (Five-Dropouts method).
architecture_details.feature_extraction: Multi-scale features are extracted by cropping the original image based on CAM thresholds (70 and 110) and resizing to 224x224 before inputting into subsequent LANets.
architecture_details.heads: Not explicitly reported as multi-head attention; uses channel and spatial attention masks.
architecture_details.classifier_head: Average of outputs from three LANets with Five-Dropouts
architecture_details.loss_function: Not explicitly reported in the provided text (standard classification loss implied)
architecture_details.training_schedule: Training monitored using the official validation set. Maximum 100 epochs with learning rate decay.

training_details.hardware: Two NVIDIA RTX 2080ti GPUs with 11GB memory
training_details.data_augmentation: Deep Data Augmentation (DDA) strategy combining traditional methods (Transpose, VerticalFlip, HorizontalFlip, Random Brightness, Random Contrast, Motion Blurring, Median Blurring, Gaussian Blurring, Gaussian Noise, Optical Distortion, Grid Distortion, Elastic Transformation, AHELC, RHSV, SSR, CutOut) and GAN inversion using StyleGAN encoder/embedding into latent space W+.

classification_or_output_head.details: Averages the predictions from three LANets. Each LANet processes a different scale of the input image (original, CAM-cropped at threshold 70, CAM-cropped at threshold 110).

preprocessing_techniques: CAM-Based Crop: Lesion regions extracted using Classification Activation Maps (CAM) obtained via self-supervised learning (MoCov2) with thresholds of 70 and 110 to remove background and obtain multi-scale views.
Resizing: Images resized to a standard size of 224x224 pixels after cropping.
Deep Data Augmentation (DDA): Combination of traditional methods and GAN inversion.

Traditional Data Augmentation: Transpose, Vertical Flip, Horizontal Flip, Random Brightness, Random Contrast, Motion Blurring, Median Blurring, Gaussian Blurring, Gaussian Noise, Optical Distortion, Grid Distortion, Elastic Transformation, Adaptive Histogram Equalization with Limited Contrast (AHELC), Random Hue Saturation Value (RHSV), Shift Scale Rotation (SSR), and CutOut.
GAN-based Augmentation: StyleGAN encoder used to embed images into extended latent space W+ for reconstruction and noise addition to generate diverse training samples.
ImageNet Pre-training: Used for backbone feature extraction (ResNet50) and initial CAM generation (ResNet18) for comparison, though self-supervised CAM was preferred for cropping.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: A combination of traditional geometric and color transforms applied via a 'black box' approach. Specific methods include: Transpose, VerticalFlip, HorizontalFlip, Random Brightness, Random Contrast, Motion Blurring, Median Blurring, Gaussian Blurring, Gaussian Noise, Optical Distortion, Grid Distortion, Elastic Transformation, Adaptive Histogram Equalization with Limited Contrast (AHELC), Random Hue Saturation Value (RHSV), Shift Scale Rotation (SSR), and CutOut.

Used StyleGAN encoder to embed images into the extended latent space W+. For each augmentation, two images of the same class were used to generate the first 7 and second 7 style vectors respectively, while the last 4 vectors were random inputs. This method reconstructs images by adding noise to the StyleGAN to enhance data diversity and robustness.

Used to extract multi-scale views of lesions without additional annotations. Classification Activation Maps (CAM) obtained via self-supervised learning (MoCov2) were used with thresholds of 70 and 110 to crop the lesion regions. The original image and two cropped versions (low threshold and high threshold) were fed into three separate LANets.

conclusions: The proposed MSLANet, a multi-scale long attention network, achieves state-of-the-art performance in skin lesion classification, outperforming existing methods without using ensemble strategies.
MSLANet achieved a rank-1 average AUC of 93.7% on the ISIC 2017 dataset and an AUC of 92.4% on the SIIM-ISIC 2020 dataset.

The integration of long attention mechanisms allows the model to fuse multi-scale information and modify low-level features using high-level semantic information, addressing challenges like inter-class similarity and intra-class variation.
Self-supervised learning using Classification Activation Maps (CAM) enables the extraction of multi-scale lesion perspectives without requiring additional manual annotations.

The proposed Deep Data Augmentation (DDA) strategy, which combines traditional augmentation with StyleGAN-based GAN inversion, significantly improves the model's generalization ability and robustness.

ImageNet pre-training was found to be the most effective pre-training method, providing better generalization ability and faster convergence compared to self-supervised methods like CMC, SimCLR, InsDis, MoCov1, and MoCov2.

While MSLANet achieves high accuracy and AUC, the multi-scale architecture introduces computational and speed limitations, with inference times significantly higher than single-scale models like ResNet50 and LANet.

The model achieves a good balance between sensitivity and specificity, which is critical for skin disease diagnosis, as focusing on a single index alone is insufficient for evaluating model performance.
important_remarks: The test set labels for the ISIC 2017 dataset were not publicly available; the official training set was split into training, validation, and test sets in a 6:1:3 ratio, which may introduce bias compared to using the official held-out test set.

The SIIM-ISIC 2020 dataset also lacked publicly available test labels, requiring the authors to split the official training set into training, validation, and test sets (6:1:3 ratio), limiting independent external validation.

MSLANet exhibits significantly higher computational cost and slower inference speed compared to single-scale models; inference time was 21.32 ms per image (batch size 1) versus 6.29 ms for ResNet50 on an RTX 2080Ti.

The model achieved high specificity (0.932 on ISIC 2017 melanoma task) but relatively low sensitivity (0.556), indicating a trade-off where the model prioritizes avoiding false positives over detecting all positive cases.

The study relies on self-supervised learning (MoCov2) to generate Classification Activation Maps (CAM) for lesion cropping without manual annotations, but this method's effectiveness is contingent on the self-supervised model's ability to focus on the lesion rather than background noise.

Deep Data Augmentation (DDA) using StyleGAN inversion was employed to address data insufficiency, but the quality of generated images and their impact on generalization to diverse clinical populations remains a limitation of synthetic data reliance.
The paper does not report statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) for performance comparisons, relying solely on point estimates of metrics like AUC and Accuracy.

The model was evaluated only on two public datasets (ISIC 2017 and SIIM-ISIC 2020); lack of validation on external, multi-center, or diverse skin-type datasets limits the assessment of real-world clinical generalizability and potential demographic bias.
